result = 0 + 3 = 3.

Node 2: 'c' is not a vowel.
Contribution = 0. result stays 3.

Node 3: 'e' is a vowel.
Contribution = (2+1)*(3-2) = 3.
result = 3 + 3 = 6.

All nodes processed.
'a' contributes 3 substrings, 'e' contributes 3.
Total = 3 + 3 = 6.

Answer: 6

def count_ops(bit_cnt, bit_sum, median_idx):
    left_cnt = bit_cnt.query(median_idx)
    left_sum = bit_sum.query(median_idx)
    right_cnt = n - left_cnt
    right_sum = bit_sum.query(m) - left_sum
    left_ops = left_cnt * median_idx - left_sum

    return left_ops + right_ops

steps.description: Filter for customers with at least 2 years of purchase history
Use correlated subquery to find the previous year's total for each row
steps.code:
CASE
    WHEN y.yr = (
        SELECT MAX(yr)
        FROM yearly y2
        WHERE y2.customer_id = y.customer_id
          AND y2.yr < y.yr

steps.description: Compare current year total with previous year total to ensure strictly increasing
steps.code:
THEN CASE
    WHEN y.total > (
        SELECT y2.total
        FROM yearly y2
        WHERE y2.customer_id = y.customer_id
          AND y2.yr = (
            SELECT MAX(yr)
            FROM yearly y3
            WHERE y3.customer_id = y.customer_id
              AND y3.yr < y.yr
          )
    ) THEN 1 ELSE 0
    END

steps.description: Wrap the CASE in SUM to count years that are strictly greater than previous year
steps.code:
SUM(
    CASE
        WHEN y.yr = (
            SELECT MAX(yr)
            FROM yearly y2
            WHERE y2.customer_id = y.customer_id
              AND y2.yr < y.yr
        )
        THEN CASE
            WHEN y.total > (
                SELECT y2.total
                FROM yearly y2
                WHERE y2.customer_id = y.customer_id
                  AND y2.yr = (
                    SELECT MAX(yr)
                    FROM yearly y3
                    WHERE y3.customer_id = y.customer_id
                      AND y3.yr < y.yr
                  )
            ) THEN 1 ELSE 0
        END
        ELSE 0
    END
) = COUNT(*) - 1

steps.description: Final complete query returning customers with strictly increasing purchases
steps.code:
WITH yearly AS (
    SELECT customer_id, YEAR(order_date) AS yr, SUM(price) AS total
    FROM Orders
    GROUP BY customer_id, YEAR(order_date)
)
SELECT y.customer_id
FROM yearly y
GROUP BY y.customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2
AND SUM(
    CASE
        WHEN y.yr = (
            SELECT MAX(yr)
            FROM yearly y2
            WHERE y2.customer_id = y.customer_id
              AND y2.yr < y.yr
        )
        THEN CASE
            WHEN y.total > (
                SELECT y2.total
                FROM yearly y2
                WHERE y2.customer_id = y.customer_id
                  AND y2.yr = (
                    SELECT MAX(yr)
                    FROM yearly y3
                    WHERE y3.customer_id = y.customer_id
                      AND y3.yr < y.yr
                  )
            ) THEN 1 ELSE 0
        END
        ELSE 0
    END
) = COUNT(*) - 1;

steps.description: Join yearly data to itself to compare consecutive years for each customer
steps.sql:
SELECT y1.customer_id
FROM yearly y1
JOIN yearly y2 ON y1.customer_id = y2.customer_id AND y1.yr = y2.yr + 1

steps.description: Filter to only include rows where current year total > previous year total
Group by customer and count how many strictly increasing transitions exist
steps.sql:
GROUP BY y1.customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) = (
  SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT yr) - 1
  FROM yearly y3
  WHERE y3.customer_id = y1.customer_id
)

steps.description: Alternative approach using MIN/MAX to check if total increases across years
steps.sql:
, stats AS (
  SELECT customer_id, MIN(yr) AS min_yr, MAX(yr) AS max_yr, COUNT(*) AS years
  FROM yearly
  GROUP BY customer_id
)

steps.description: Join stats with yearly to compare first and last year totals
steps.sql:
SELECT s.customer_id
FROM stats s
JOIN yearly y_first ON s.customer_id = y_first.customer_id AND y_first.yr = s.min_yr
JOIN yearly y_last ON s.customer_id = y_last.customer_id AND y_last.yr = s.max_yr

steps.description: Check that last year total exceeds first year total
Add condition that customer must have at least 2 years of data
Use anti-join to exclude customers with any year-over-year decrease
steps.sql:
NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM yearly y3
  JOIN yearly y4 ON y3.customer_id = y4.customer_id AND y3.yr = y4.yr + 1
  WHERE y3.total <= y4.total
)

steps.description: Final query selects customer_ids meeting all strictly increasing criteria
steps.sql:
SELECT s.customer_id
FROM stats s
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM yearly y
  WHERE y.customer_id = s.customer_id
  AND EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM yearly y2
    WHERE y2.customer_id = y.customer_id
    AND y2.yr = y.yr - 1
    AND y2.total >= y.total
  )
)
AND s.years >= 2;

steps.description: Add LAG window function to get previous year's total
steps.code:
ordered AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        yr,
        total,
        LAG(total) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY yr) AS prev_total
    FROM yearly
)

steps.description: Filter for strictly increasing records (current > previous)
steps.code:
increased AS (
    SELECT customer_id
    FROM ordered
    WHERE total > prev_total
)

steps.description: Group by customer and ensure all their years are strictly increasing
steps.code:
SELECT customer_id
FROM (
    SELECT
        o.customer_id,
        COUNT(*) AS years_increased,
        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM yearly y WHERE y.customer_id = o.customer_id) - 1 AS total_transitions
    FROM ordered o
    WHERE o.total > o.prev_total
    GROUP BY o.customer_id
) c
WHERE years_increased = total_transitions
AND total_transitions > 0
